coredata.dc:title: Pouch lithium-ion battery thermal management by using a new liquid-cooling plate with honeycomb-like fins
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Linxiang
Zhang, Zhendong
Sheng, Lei
Kuang, Zhiwei
Zhu, Zehua
Bi, Qing
coredata.dc:description: Excellent thermal management plays a significant role in ensuring lithium-ion batteries' performances. This work proposes a thermal control method for pouch batteries by using a cooling-plate with novel channels designed with streamlined and honeycomb-like fins. Numerically, such effects are studied as coolant mass flow, inlet temperature, cooling-plate's main channel aspect ratio, and fin spacing on the battery's thermal performance. An optimal scheme for comprehensive performance of the battery is selected by orthogonal test method. The results show that increasing coolant mass flow can suppress the battery's temperature rise. Moreover, the pressure drop of cooling-plate increases linearly. Decreasing coolant inlet temperature has a positive impact on reducing battery temperature rise and minimizing temperature difference. Increasing the main channel aspect ratio and fin spacing of cooling-plate both reduce the battery's minimum temperature, but the uniformity of coolant flow distribution is improved and deteriorated, respectively. The orthogonally optimized scheme (A5B2C2D3) can control maximum cell temperature at 27.29 °C while reducing pressure drop by up to 53.71 %. Experimental validation shows that the designed cooling-plate has excellent cooling performance, and the maximum temperature deviation is within 2.00 °C. The study would be valuable to deeply understand the thermal design progress of battery cooling-plate.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pouch lithium-ion battery
Thermal management
Liquid cooling-plate
Orthogonal experimental design

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Calibration results of simulated battery model's heat generation rate.
Battery module pattern.
Independence analysis for model: (a) Grid number; (b)Time step.

Effects of different channel designs: (a) Average cell temperature; (b) Minimum cell temperature; (c) Cell temperature difference (at 0.7C, 1.0C and 1.2C); (d) Thermal distribution of battery, LCP and fluid domain (at 1.0C).
Cloud maps of different channel designs: (a) Pressure in channels and velocity vectors in fluid domain; (b) Vorticity magnitude in LCP-C's vortex core region.
Effects of different coolant mass flow G: (a) Maximum cell temperature; (b) Cell temperature difference; (c) Pressure drop and heat exchange capacity (at 1.0C).
Effects of different coolant inlet temperature T liq: (a) Maximum cell temperature; (b) Cell temperature difference; (c) Pressure drop and heat exchange capacity (at 1.0C).
Effects of different main channel aspect ratio w/h: (a) Cell temperature; (b) Pressure drop, heat exchange capacity, flow uniformity and mass of LCP (at 1.0C).
Effects of different fin spacing L: (a) Cell temperature; (b) Pressure drop, heat exchange capacity, flow uniformity and mass of LCP (at 1.0C).
Effect curve of each performance objective on four factors: (a) Main channel aspect ratio w/h; (b) Fin spacing L; (c) Coolant inlet temperature T liq; (d) Coolant flow rate G.
Performance comparison of different performance objectives.
Performance comparison of different schemes.
Experiment setup for thermal test (a) Schematic diagram for the test system; (b) Actual test system for battery module.

Temperature history comparison between experiment and simulation: (a) Average temperature of heating module and actual battery; Obtained cell temperature variations from the simulation and test (b) 0.7C; (c) 1.0C; (d) 1.2C.
Summary of recent research progress focusing on liquid cooling BTMS.

head.ce:title: Pouch lithium-ion battery thermal management by using a new liquid-cooling plate with honeycomb-like fins

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200093, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Shanghai for Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical Engineering, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai 200093, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Changzhou Shineworld Intelligent Manufacturing Technology Company Limited, Changzhou, 213000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Changzhou Shineworld Intelligent Manufacturing Technology Company Limited

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Changzhou Shineworld Intelligent Manufacturing Technology Company Limited, Changzhou 213000, P.R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Mechanical Engineering, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, 516 Jungong Road, Yangpu District, Shanghai, 200093, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Shanghai for Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Mechanical Engineering, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, 516 Jungong Road, Yangpu District, Shanghai, 200093, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Shanghai for Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Excellent thermal management plays a significant role in ensuring lithium-ion batteries' performances. This work proposes a thermal control method for pouch batteries by using a cooling-plate with novel channels designed with streamlined and honeycomb-like fins. Numerically, such effects are studied as coolant mass flow, inlet temperature, cooling-plate's main channel aspect ratio, and fin spacing on the battery's thermal performance. An optimal scheme for comprehensive performance of the battery is selected by orthogonal test method. The results show that increasing coolant mass flow can suppress the battery's temperature rise. Moreover, the pressure drop of cooling-plate increases linearly. Decreasing coolant inlet temperature has a positive impact on reducing battery temperature rise and minimizing temperature difference. Increasing the main channel aspect ratio and fin spacing of cooling-plate both reduce the battery's minimum temperature, but the uniformity of coolant flow distribution is improved and deteriorated, respectively. The orthogonally optimized scheme (A5B2C2D3) can control maximum cell temperature at 27.29 °C while reducing pressure drop by up to 53.71 %. Experimental validation shows that the designed cooling-plate has excellent cooling performance, and the maximum temperature deviation is within 2.00 °C. The study would be valuable to deeply understand the thermal design progress of battery cooling-plate.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Nomenclature

row.entry: Heat flux density of battery surface (W∙m−2)
Width of longitudinal section of main channel of LCP (m)
Hight of longitudinal section of main channel of LCP (m)

ce:section.ce:para: At present, the rapid development of electric vehicles has expanded the application of lithium-ion batteries from 3C (Computer, Communication, Consumer Electronics) electronic products to automotive power drive systems. The battery size has increased, and energy density has gradually improved [1,2]. The increasing demand from customers for high-rate charging and discharging of batteries poses a huge challenge to the widespread promotion of electric vehicle industry. Generally, as a nonlinear time-varying system, the output performance of power battery systems is highly susceptible to external temperature interference, which affects the physical parameters inside the battery [3] (internal resistance, voltage, and current), ultimately causing significant fluctuations in the battery's state of charge (SOC). Among them, in low-temperature environments, the chemical reaction activity of lithium-ion batteries decreases, the charge transfer rate decreases, resulting in a significant decrease in the battery's SOC, power, and charging rate, and an extension of storage life. When the high-temperature environment stimulates, the chemical reaction rate inside the battery increases, and the available capacity increases [4]. However, the battery temperature will rise rapidly, and electric vehicles are prone to thermal runaway incidents such as explosions and fires, endangering personal and property safety [5–7]. In addition, the heat generation rate of battery is positively correlated with the battery temperature [8], and the discharge rate will accelerate the battery temperature rise [9]. Overall, lithium-ion batteries have high temperature sensitivity, especially during high rate charging and discharging. When heat accumulates inside the battery pack, battery module will produce more adverse temperature rise, shorten the service life of new energy vehicles, and reduce the vehicle's driving safety performance [10]. Therefore, to regulate the temperature variations of large-scale lithium-ion battery modules under high-rate discharge in real time and achieve vehicle energy management and safety warning, it is crucial to design an appropriate battery thermal management system (BTMS) [11,12].

In recent years, three main cooling methods for BTMS have emerged: air cooling [13–15], phase change material (PCM) cooling [16–18], and liquid cooling [19,20]. Air cooling BTMS utilizes gas as a cooling medium, with a simple structure and easy maintenance [21]. Nevertheless, it has poor noise, vibration, and harshness (NVH) characteristics, as well as limited heat dissipation capacity, making it challenging to satisfy the cooling demands of battery packs under high-load conditions [22]. Furthermore, PCM cooling BTMS commonly employs paraffin and other PCMs as thermal management media, providing strong heat dissipation [23]. However, when the PCM is in a molten state, the battery pack requires a high level of sealing, which increases manufacturing costs [24]. Comparatively, liquid cooling BTMS uses liquid as cooling media, and the system usually contains components with internal cooling channels, such as liquid cooling plates (LCPs), jackets [25], or heat pipes [26]. These parts can be flexibly arranged between batteries or placed on the sidewalls, top, or bottom surface of the battery pack. The coolant circulates within these components, which can indirectly dissipate heat from the battery module. In addition, the battery module can also be directly immersed in a highly insulated and thermally conductive coolant, which can directly dissipate heat from the battery module [27]. Liquid cooling BTMS, with its rapid cooling rate, excellent NVH characteristics, and compact structure, is suitable for battery packs with high cooling demands and has become a research focus in the academic community. Currently, for indirect liquid cooling BTMS, scholars primarily focus on the design and optimization of LCPs' channel structures, with common channel structures including serpentine channels [28], parallel multi-channels [29], microchannels [30], and bionic/topological optimization channels [31,32].

Each of the serpentine channel LCPs has an inlet and outlet without branching in the flow direction. Mubashir et al. [33] placed the serpentine channel LCPs between two 10Ah batteries for heat dissipation, and found that increasing the number of serpentine channels within the battery module can improve cooling performance. Additionally, a dual-serpentine LCP design reduces the temperature difference of battery by 41 %. Sakkera et al. [34] added rectangular or triangular grooves on the serpentine channel heat sink to disrupt the thermal boundary layer. The Nusselt number and Reynolds number increased, with an average thermal enhancement efficiency of up to 12 %. But the pressure drop increased by 56. Zhang et al. [35] added secondary openings and Y-shaped fins to the serpentine LCP. This integrated design can control the average temperature of battery module within 44.2 °C, while reducing the pressure drop by 33.34 %, improving the overall performance of LCP. Jiang et al. [36] subsequently applied a Bayesian optimization algorithm to optimize the structure of serpentine channel LCPs, and pointed out that channel depth affects the heat dissipation performance of battery modules. Meanwhile, changes in channel width and turning radius reduce pumping power by up to 71 %. Obviously, the longer flow path and numerous turns in serpentine channel LCPs can generate vortices, leading to a larger pressure drop and increased pump power consumption.

Therefore, shunting at the inlet of LCP and arranging multiple straight or curved channel structures parallel to LCP can effectively reduce the pumping power. Sheng et al. [37] used rectangular multi-channel LCPs to cool battery modules from both the large and small sides, respectively, and found that cooling from the small side can effectively regulate the battery temperature, while the mass of LCP was reduced by 57 %. Chen et al. [38] introduced a bidirectional symmetric parallel channel LCP suitable for battery packs, which reduced the energy consumption of the liquid cooling system by 82 %. Amalesh et al. [39] developed a parallel 7-channel LCP with straight, sine wave, and zigzag baselines, and found that the channel baseline profile significantly improved the cooling performance of battery module, with optimal temperature uniformity between the cells maintained within 2 °C. However, the channel pressure drop is improved by one-third.

In traditional parallel straight-channel LCPs, the axial convective heat transfer gradually weakens. With increasing battery capacity, scholars have designed LCPs with mini-channels or fin structures [40] to further enhance the thermal performance of battery module cooling systems by increasing heat exchange area. Rao et al. [41] and Huo et al. [42] numerically investigated the effect of parallel mini-channel LCPs on the battery temperature field, and pointed out that mini-channel design can effectively suppress the battery's maximum temperature. Then, Huang et al. [43] optimized the straight channels into streamlined channels based on the concept of ‘high efficiency and low resistance’, which significantly reduced flow resistance and improved thermal management performance. Ren et al. [44] and Hassan et al. [45] used a hybrid cooling system to dissipate heat during high-rate operation of battery modules. They pointed out that at low discharge rates, passive cooling of PCM alone can meet the heat dissipation requirements. When discharge rate increases, a mini-channels active liquid cooling system must be coupled to compensate for heat dissipation. Li et al. [46] studied the heat dissipation effect of mini-channel cooling plates on cylindrical batteries operating at different ambient temperatures, and pointed out that at extreme high temperatures, temperature uniformity of the thermal system can be improved by gradient reduction of coolant inlet temperature and flow rate. Wei et al. [47] and Jin et al. [48] added slant-cut fins within LCPs, which significantly improved the heat dissipation efficiency of the thermal system, and the average battery temperature remained below 44.31 °C.

Alternatively, based on biomimicry and system topology optimization design approaches, scholars have investigated new channel-structured LCPs to further improve the thermal performance of LCPs and reduce pressure drop. Liu et al. [49] developed a tree-like mini-channel heat sink for cooling prismatic batteries during 4C discharge. Compared with straight mini-channel, the heat exchange capacity of optimized plate is improved, and the temperature difference of battery can be reduced to 4.86 °C, but the pressure drop will increase reasonably. Liu et al. [50] and Chen et al. [51] designed LCPs with bionic leaf vein branches and lotus leaf channels, respectively. And pointed out that bionic leaf vein channel design can control the maximum temperature of battery during 3C discharge at 33.34 °C, while the lotus leaf bionic design can reduce pressure drop by 44.35 % and improve the temperature accumulation problem during lithium-ion battery discharge. Wang et al. [52] studied the thermal characteristics of pouch batteries under low-temperature discharge conditions, and pointed out that the channel width of bionic spider-web channel cooling plate significantly affects the cooling performance, while channel angle has the smallest effect, but temperature uniformity on the battery surface is improved. Ren et al. [53] designed biomimetic and topological LCPs, respectively, and found that biomimetic design is slightly superior to topological design, but convective heat transfer is significantly enhanced compared to traditional cooling plate structures, and no significant pressure drop is generated. Yuan et al. [54] obtained a liquid cooling system using a topology optimization method, which reduced the pressure drop by 21.20 %. Wang et al. [55] studied the impact of different goal functions on the topology optimization design of channels. They found that the cooling performance of LCP is optimal when outlet enthalpy is maximized, and the system's energy use efficiency is improved when channel performance is prioritized.

Summarizing the existing research, as shown in Table 1 . Previous studies have focused mainly on prismatic and cylindrical medium/small capacity batteries, with relatively limited research on large-scale pouch lithium-ion batteries. Moreover, the LCP's channel pattern design is relatively single, focusing on optimizing parallel microchannels, serpentine, biomimetic, and topological channels, or combining these with other cooling methods, rarely is attention given to couple multiple channel design methods, within the LCP itself. Therefore, it is necessary to design an efficient and economical BTMS to control the temperature of battery module within a reasonable range in response to the undesirable temperature rise behavior of pouch batteries during high-rate discharge.

This study presents a novel LCP with honeycomb-like fins for pouch lithium-ion batteries’ thermal management. The LCP channel structure consists of five parallel streamlined main channels and a secondary channel connected by fin structures. The effects of LCP channel pattern and cooling parameters on the battery module were analyzed numerically. Through orthogonal design, the influence factors on the performance objective are thoroughly analyzed, and the best matching scheme for the comprehensive performance of battery modules is selected. A self-built thermal test system served for authentication of the simulation effectiveness.

A 55Ah large-format pouch lithium-ion battery with cathode material Li (Ni0.5Co0.2Mn0.3) O2 and anode material graphite, serves as the research object. Table 2 lists the specification parameters of the battery.

Based on the study by Sheng et al. [57], a macroscopic theoretical model for battery heat generation was adopted, and the battery's heat generation rate was tested using the ‘calibration calorimetric method’. The theoretical model of battery heat generation is as follows,
(1)
q
( t )
=
c cell
m cell
d
T cell
( t )
d t
+
A cell
q ‾
( t )
where c and m are specific heat and mass, respectively; T is temperature; t is time; and subscript ‘cell’ indicates battery. A cell is cell's superficial area;
q ‾
( t )
is the heat flux density of battery surface. Thus, based on the rate of temperature increase and the heat flux density on battery surface, the fitting equations [56] for the heat generation rate of lithium-ion batteries at various discharge rates can be derived, as shown in Table 3 .

To ensure the reliability of thermal simulation results, after completion of the battery heat generation rate test, it is necessary to calibrate the heat generation rate of simulated battery model and obtain the average temperature comparison results between the simulated battery and real battery, as shown in Fig. 1 .

As shown in Fig. 1, average temperature rise of the simulated battery model is basically consistent with the temperature rise trend of real battery, with a maximum temperature deviation of no more than 1.49 °C and a maximum relative deviation of less than 3.98 %, indicating that the accuracy of simulated battery model is high and can lay a foundation for subsequent modeling and simulation research.

The battery module consists of several single cells connected in series. The module adopts a sandwiched structure, with one LCP placed between each pair of adjacent cells. The simplification process of the battery module is shown in Fig. 2 .

Design techniques using 3D modeling a simplified battery model consisting of two half cells (300 × 100 × 5.6 mm3) and a cooling plate (300 × 100 × 6 mm3) was constructed, as shown in Fig. 2. The LCP includes three types of channel structures, labeled LCP-A, LCP-B, and LCP-C.

The channel pattern of LCP-A refers to the streamlined channel design schemes proposed by Zhang [56], Qian [41], Huang [43] et al. Firstly, the LCP of cavity is modeled, and then the simulation is solved based on boundary conditions such as coolant mass flow inlet and pressure outlet. Finally, five streamlines are extracted, the width of these streamlines is expanded, and the channel pattern of LCP-A is obtained. Five channels are evenly distributed along the width of the LCP, and entire plate is symmetric about both the axial and radial centerlines. The cross-sectional area of channels is 6 × 4 mm2, with an aspect ratio (w/h) of 3/2. The radians of internal and external channels are 0.49 rad and 1.03 rad, respectively.

A series of uniformly arranged, honeycomb-like fins are placed in LCP-B. The size of fins is determined based on the radial distance between each channel in LCP-A, with the axial distance (L) between the fins being 6 mm.

The fluid domain of LCP-C is divided into a main channel and sub-channel. The main channel follows the streamlined design of LCP-A, while each main channel is connected by whole or partial honeycomb-like fins, similar to LCP-B, forming the sub-channel.

The LCP is made of aluminum alloy 6003, and the coolant used is a 50 % glycol solution (GS) with 20.0 °C, 22.5 °C, 25.0 °C, 27.5 °C, 30.0 °C. Table 4 presents the main performance parameters of LCP and coolant [58,59].

The charging and discharging of lithium-ion batteries involve heat and mass transfer processes, accompanied by heat generation and dissipation. The energy governing equation for the battery can be denoted as,
(2)
ρ cell
c cell
∂
T cell
∂ t
= −
λ cell
∇ 2
T cell
+
q ·
Energy governing equation of the LCP is denoted as [60],
(3)
∂
∂ t
(
ρ lcp
c lcp
T lcp
)
= − ∇
(
λ lcp
∇
T lcp
)
where subscript ‘lcp’ indicates LCP.

The mass, energy and momentum governing equations for the fluid in LCP are expressed as [37],
(4)
∂
ρ liq
∂ t
+ ∇
(
ρ liq
u
)
= 0
(5)

T liq
)
+ ∇
(

u liq
T liq
)
= − ∇
(

T liq
)
(6)
∂

(
ρ liq
u
)
+ ∇
(
ρ liq
u u
)
= − ∇ P
where u is coolant's velocity; the subscript ‘liq’ indicates coolant; P is liquid statics pressure.

For multi-channel LCPs, the standard deviation (S) is commonly used to evaluate the degree of flow distribution uniformity across the channels. The smaller the value, the better flow distribution uniformity.
(7)
S =
1
N − 1
∑
i = 1
N
(
G i
G avg
− 1
)
2
(
i = 1 , 2 , ⋯ , N
)
where N is the total number of flow channels (N = 5), G i is the mass flow of fluid in each channel, and G avg is the fluid's average mass flow in each channel.

The battery model is meshed and numerically solved using CFD software. The initial temperature of the model is 25 °C. The smaller sides of the battery and LCP are set as natural convection boundaries, with a convective heat transfer coefficient of 1.75 W/(m2·°C) [61]. The inlet and outlet boundary conditions of the LCP are set as a mass flow inlet and a pressure outlet, respectively. In LCP-A, the maximum Reynolds number of the fluid below 270, a laminar flow model is selected and solved using the SIMPLEC algorithm. Under these conditions, the fluid flows in a regular, uniform, and layered pattern along the geometric channel of LCP-A, the simulation results are stable and convergent. Therefore, the model selection is reasonable [56]. In contrast, in LCP-B and LCP-C, it is difficult to calculate the necessary parameters (such as characteristic length) required for solving the Reynolds number. Additionally, the geometric structure of fins inside the LCP significantly affects the fluid flow, generating vortices. Considering both high and low flow velocities of fluid, both the k-ε turbulence and laminar flow models were explored. It was found that the simulation results using the k-ε turbulence model were more stable and uniquely converged, so k-ε turbulence model was ultimately chosen.

To ensure the accuracy of numerical simulation, a grid and time step independence analysis is necessary. This study mainly involves the solution of three initial models, and we chose to synchronize the initialization of non-steady state and independence simulations. Specifically, the initialization results for non-steady state simulation of LCP-A are referenced from Ref. [56]. Under the same grid size, a non-steady state simulation of LCP-C was carried out, with a battery discharge rate of 1.0C and a coolant flow rate of 0.4 × 10−3 kg s−1, the maximum cell temperature (T max) and pressure drop (Δp) of the LCP-C at the end of discharge are shown in Fig. 3 .

In Fig. 3, as the number of grids increases from 1.71 million to 7.23 million and the time step increases from 0.5 s to 2.0 s, the trend of pressure drop in the LCP and maximum cell temperature is opposite. When the grid numbers are 5.13 million and 7.23 million, and the time steps are 0.5 s and 1.0 s, the changes in pressure drop and temperature are relatively small. Therefore, to ensure the accuracy and stability of the calculation while shortening solving time, meeting the computational cost, a grid count of 5.13 million and a time step of 1.0 s are selected for analysis in this study.

With an initial model temperature of 25 °C and a mass flow at 0.4 × 10−3 kg s−1 of 50 % GS, the simulation results for the battery module with three different channel designs (LCP-A, LCP-B, and LCP-C) during 0.7C, 1.0C and 1.2C discharge are presented in Figs. 4 and 5
.

In Fig. 4 (a), (b), and (c), As the battery discharge deepens, battery's heat generation rate continuously rises, and the cooling plate's heat dissipation rate slowly reaches its peak. When heat generation and dissipation rates remain balanced (1000–3000 s), the minimum cell temperature remains constant, while average and maximum cell temperatures continue to rise steadily. At the end of discharge, due to the rise in internal resistance of battery, the battery temperature increases significantly. In addition, as the discharge rate increases, the temperature rise of battery becomes more pronounced, and the balance time between heat generation rate and heat dissipation rate becomes shorter. Overall, when the battery is discharged at different rates, all three liquid cooling systems can maintain the battery temperature between 26.00 °C and 32.76 °C. Among them, LCP-C keeps battery temperature at the lowest level (26.00 °C). This is because fin structure increases the heat exchange area of coolant, leading to an increase in heat exchange capacity and significantly affecting the minimum cell temperature. However, since the heat exchange capacity of LCP is limited, the maximum cell temperature does not change significantly. Therefore, the cooling system of LCP-C results in a larger temperature difference and temperature field standard deviation at a certain moment in the battery, which is opposite to the trend of cell temperature changes. Moreover, the temperature uniformity of battery module is significantly weakened with the increase of discharge rate. When the battery is discharged to the end at 0.7C, 1.0C, and 1.2C, the cell temperature field standard deviations are 1.43 °C, 2.28 °C, and 2.85 °C, respectively. Furthermore, Fig. 4(d) shows that the temperature of battery, cooling-plate, and fluid domain gradually rises along the coolant flow direction, while they gradually drop along the heat transfer path (battery-cooling plate-fluid domain).

Fig. 5 (a) shows that pressure in the cooling-plate gradually decreases along the direction of coolant flow, with the highest pressure at the inlet and the lowest at the outlet. Among them, LCP-B has the highest pressure drop (6.95 Pa), followed by LCP-A (5.94 Pa), and LCP-C has the lowest pressure drop (4.61 Pa). Additionally, due to turbulent structures within LCP-B and LCP-C, the direction of coolant flow changes, disrupting the boundary layer, and leading to the generation of vortices (shown in Fig. 5 (b)). Extract the vortex core and monitor the vorticity (vorticity magnitude) values of velocity field by post-processing methods. Fig. 5(b) shows that the vortex core region is symmetrically distributed based on the LCP axis, with a vorticity magnitude of 10 s−1. Generally, vortices enhanced heat transfer, turbulence energy and fluid velocity. Besides, because LCP-C has secondary flow channels, coolant can flow between them, allowing the flow rate of coolant within the cooling-plate to balance automatically. As a result, LCP-C has the best flow uniformity of coolant distribution (S = 0.024).

In summary, compared with LCP-A and LCP-B, using LCP-C to cool battery modules results in a decrease in minimum cell temperature by 29.68 % and 5.16 %, a reduction in pressure drop by 22.52 % and 33.70 %, a reduction in mass by 29.74 % and 11.61 %, and an improvement in flow uniformity by 48.13 % and 63.10 %. However, the cell temperature difference increases by 16.07 % and 2.24 %, respectively. Quantitative analysis shows that LCP-C has good heat dissipation and pressure drop performance. Therefore, a quantitative numerical study of operating conditions for the LCP-C cooling system and the plate's structural parameters is conducted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of cooling system operating conditions

ce:section.ce:para: Using 50 % GS as the coolant, the initial temperature of model and coolant inlet is set to 25.0 °C. The main channel aspect ratio is 3/2, and fin spacing is 6 mm. And the coolant mass flow gradually increases from 0.2 × 10−3 kg s−1 to 1.0 × 10−3 kg s−1. The numerical simulation results under these conditions are shown in Fig. 6 .

In Fig. 6, the maximum temperature and temperature difference of the battery drop with the rise in coolant mass flow, but the decreasing trend slows down. The maximum temperature and temperature difference of battery initial discharge increase significantly, and after approximately 1200 s, the battery's heat generation rate balances with the heat dissipation rate of liquid cooling system, resulting in minor changes in the maximum cell temperature and temperature difference. Additionally, the higher the flow rate, the longer the equilibrium time. However, due to the limited heat dissipation capacity of the cooling-plate, when the heat exchange capacity reaches its peak, even if the mass flow continues to increase, the maximum temperature and temperature difference of the battery will not change significantly, showing marginal effects. At this point, the LCP's pressure drop increases linearly. According to engineering experience, a liquid cooling system typically needs to control the maximum cell temperature and temperature difference within 32.0 °C and 5.0 °C, respectively. When mass flow is 0.4 × 10−3 kg s−1, the cell's maximum temperature and temperature difference are 31.13 °C and 4.55 °C, respectively, while the temperature field standard deviation is 2.27 °C, meeting the thermal design requirements of battery module. Moreover, the inlet and outlet pressure drop of cooling-plate is 4.61 Pa, and the heat exchange capacity of cooling-plate is 14.27 kJ. Therefore, a coolant mass flow of 0.4 × 10−3 kg s−1 offers cost advantages for cooling large-scale pouch battery modules, provided that thermal targets are met.

The mass flow of 50 % GS is 0.4 × 10−3 kg s−1, with a main channel aspect ratio of 3/2 and a fin spacing of 6 mm. The coolant inlet temperature rises from 20.0 °C to 30.0 °C. Numerical simulation results under these conditions are shown in Fig. 7 .

In Fig. 7(a) and (b), the trends of maximum temperature and temperature difference of battery at the end of discharge are opposite under different coolant inlet temperatures. When the coolant inlet temperature is lower than ambient temperature (T liq < T a = 25.0 °C), at the initial stage of battery discharge, the cooling system efficiently cools the battery module, causing a significant fall in battery temperature and an abrupt rise in temperature difference. Subsequently, as the battery's heat generation rate gradually increases, maximum cell temperature rises while minimum temperature remains constant, resulting in an increased temperature difference. When the battery's heat generation rate approaches the heat dissipation rate of liquid cooling system, cooling performance reaches its maximum and tends to stabilize (1500–2700 s), resulting in a relatively stable battery temperature and temperature difference. If the battery's heat generation rate continues to increase, battery temperature and temperature difference will reach their peak values at the end of discharge. When coolant inlet temperature is 22.5 °C, maximum cell temperature and temperature difference are 29.51 °C and 5.30 °C, respectively. When the coolant inlet temperature is higher than ambient temperature (T liq > T a = 25.0 °C), the “cold-side” battery can cool coolant, causing the liquid cooling system to fail. The battery's heat generation rate will exceed the heat dissipation rate of cooling-plate, resulting in a gradual increase in battery temperature and temperature difference. As coolant inlet and outlet temperatures approach the minimum cell temperature, temperature difference of the battery will slowly decrease. As discharge deepens, the cooling system becomes effective, suppressing the temperature rise of battery, and the temperature difference increases. When coolant inlet temperature is 27.5 °C, the maximum cell temperature and temperature difference are 33.56 °C and 3.81 °C, respectively.

In Fig. 7 (c), as coolant inlet temperature increases, liquid concentration and viscosity decrease, reducing the frictional drag within the cooling-plate, which causes pressure drop to linearly decrease to 4.03 Pa. At the same time, the overall heat exchange capacity of cooling-plate decreases. However, when the coolant inlet temperature is 30.0 °C, the prolonged failure time of cooling system causes fluctuations in coolant inlet and outlet temperatures, thereby enhancing the heat exchange capacity of cooling-plate. Therefore, a coolant inlet temperature of 25 °C is selected as more appropriate to meet the thermal objective.

The initial temperature of the model is set to 25.0 °C, with 50 % GS as coolant, and the mass flow of 0.4 × 10−3 kg s−1. The value range for main channel aspect ratio and fin spacing is shown in Table 5 .

In Fig. 8 , as main channel aspect ratio increases, the trends of maximum and minimum cell temperatures are opposite, with the average temperature first increasing and then decreasing. The maximum temperature rise of the battery module is 0.12 °C. When the aspect ratio is 4/2, the maximum and average cell temperatures reach their peak values, at 31.16 °C and 29.55 °C, respectively. Additionally, the temperature difference and temperature field standard deviation of the battery increase with the increase in aspect ratio. With the same mass flow rate, as the main channel aspect ratio increases, heat exchange area inside the cooling-plate increases, enhancing the heat exchange capacity of coolant, reaching a maximum of 14.35 kJ. Meanwhile, as the aspect ratio increases, the liquid flow velocity decreases, significantly reducing frictional drag within the cooling-plate. Consequently, the pressure drop gradually decreases to 2.53 Pa. Due to the increase in aspect ratio, the mutual rates of coolant between main channels tend to stabilize, allowing coolant to evenly fill each channel. The flow uniformity of coolant distribution is improved, with a minimum value of 0.016. Additionally, as aspect ratio increases, the mass of cooling-plate decreases, which meets the lightweight design requirements for automotive components.

In Fig. 9 , as fin spacing expands, the minimum and average temperatures of battery gradually decrease, while the trend of maximum cell temperature is opposite. The maximum temperature rise of the battery module is 0.10 °C. When fin spacing is set at 12 mm, the average and minimum cell temperatures are 29.53 °C and 26.48 °C, respectively, while the maximum temperature is 31.16 °C. Additionally, the temperature difference and temperature field standard deviation of battery increase with the rise in fin spacing. When fin spacing is set at 12 mm, the temperature difference and standard deviation are 4.68 °C and 2.34 °C, respectively. As fin spacing expands, the heat exchange area inside cooling-plate increases, enhancing the heat exchange capacity of coolant, which can rise to 14.42 kJ. At the same time, the frictional drag within cooling-plate decreases, reducing the pressure drop to 3.94 Pa. However, the overall change in pressure drop is not significant. This is because the primary flow direction of coolant within the cooling-plate is axial. Although secondary channels formed by the fins can alter the longitudinal interaction intensity of coolant, their impact on heat dissipation and pressure drop performance of the cooling-plate is relatively weak. As fin spacing increases, the longitudinal interaction of coolant intensifies, leading to a decrease in the flow uniformity of coolant distribution between main channels, with a maximum value of 0.038. Therefore, when designing the thermal management system, it is essential to select appropriate cooling-plate structural parameters and system operating conditions to ensure economical and efficient operation of the thermal system.

Using a control variable method to discuss the impact of a single factor on battery module performance has certain limitations, as it cannot demonstrate the comprehensive effects of multiple factors. Orthogonal experimental design is commonly used to study practical problems involving multi-factors and multi-levels, which can not only uniformly screen representative experimental schemes but also reduce the number of experiments. This method can comprehensively consider the influence of multiple factors on performance objectives and find the optimal matching scheme for each factor with high accuracy [62]. This study considers main channel aspect ratio (w/h), fin spacing (L), coolant inlet temperature (T liq), and coolant flow rate (G) as influencing factors. The values of each orthogonal factor and level are shown in Table 6 .

Based on four factors and five levels, the orthogonal array of L25(56) is used (shown in Table 7 ). The first column represents the experiment sequence number, and the first row contains level values of all factors set for experiment 1 (abbreviated as Exp. 1). For example, the scheme with w/h of 1/2, L of 4 mm, T liq of 20.0 °C, and G of 0.2 × 10−3 kg s−1 is denoted as A1B1C1D1. In this study, the performance objectives of experiments are the maximum cell temperature (T max), temperature field standard deviation (T σ), and pressure drop across the plate (Δp). The simulation results are shown in Table 7.

After completing the orthogonal experiment, perform range analysis and variance analysis on the simulation results. Use F-tests to decompose the data into factor levels and experimental error, and determine the significance of each factor.

Tables 8 and 9
indicate that, with the maximum cell temperature as objective, the coolant inlet temperature (CFactor) has the highest range and variance values, followed by coolant mass flow (DFactor) and main channel aspect ratio (AFactor). The fin spacing (BFactor) has the lowest range and variance values. With the cell temperature field standard deviation as objective, CFactor has the highest range and variance values, followed by AFactor. DFactor and BFactor have the lowest range and variance values. With the pressure drop as objective, AFactor has the highest range and variance values, followed by DFactor. CFactors and BFactors have the lowest range and variance values. Additionally, according to F-tests, the significance order of each influencing factor for different performance objectives is as follows: CFactor > DFactor > AFactor > BFactor, CFactor > AFactor > DFactor > BFactor, and AFactor > DFactor > CFactor > BFactor. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 10 , as the values of CFactor and DFactor increase, T max increases and decreases, respectively, T σ decreases overall, and Δp increases overall. As AFactor and BFactor increase, Δp decreases and increases, respectively. Since smaller values for the three performance objectives indicate better heat dissipation, temperature field uniformity, and pressure drop performance for battery module. So initially selected optimal schemes for each single performance objective are as follows: for maximum cell temperature, A3B4C1D5 (labeled as No. 26); for temperature field standard deviation, A3B2C5D5 and A3B4C5D5 (labeled as No. 27 and No. 28); and for pressure drop, A5B1C1D1 (labeled as No. 29).

Comparing the simulation results of four single-objective optimization schemes (No. 26, 27, 28, and 29) (shown in Fig. 11 ), it is observed that when T max is minimized (23.56 °C), the pressure drop increases to 12.69 Pa; when T
σ is minimized (1.36 °C), the pressure drop is 10.88 Pa; and when Δp is minimized (1.51 Pa), T max is 30.19 °C. This clearly illustrates that optimizing multiple performance objectives can result in the improvement of one metric at the expense of others. To find the optimal matching scheme, a comprehensive balance method is used to further analyze the overall performance of battery module.

Factor AFactor: It not only significantly affects pressure drop, but also impacts the maximum cell temperature and temperature field standard deviation. When AFactor increases from 3/2 to 5/2, to balance the overall performance of thermal management system, it is necessary to minimize the plate's mass as much as possible while reducing the battery's maximum temperature and pressure drop. When AFactor is 5/2, the pressure drop, maximum cell temperature, and standard deviation are reduced by 45.90 %, 10.92 %, and 7.14 %, respectively, compared with their maximum values.

Factor BFactor: For three groups of objectives, all are minor factors. When BFactor is set at 6 mm, maximum cell temperature, standard deviation and pressure drop are reduced by 12.00 %, 11.63 %, and 17.11 %, respectively, compared with their maximum values.

Factor CFactor: It not only significantly affects the maximum cell temperature, but also impacts the temperature field standard deviation. When CFactor is set at 22.5 °C, maximum cell temperature, standard deviation and pressure drop are reduced by 65.13 %, 10.61 %, and 20.56 %, respectively, compared with their maximum values.

Factor DFactor: It not only affects the maximum cell temperature, but also impacts the pressure drop. When DFactor is 0.6 × 10−3 kg s−1, maximum cell temperature, pressure drop, and standard deviation are reduced by 21.32 %, 39.63 %, and 7.93 %, respectively, compared with their maximum values. Therefore, after balancing various objectives, the determined optimal combination scheme is A5B2C2D3, labeled as No. 30.

After numerical solution, the performance objectives of No. 30 are compared with those of all schemes in Table 7, with the maximum cell temperature below 32.00 °C and temperature difference below 5.00 °C. This comparison involves 14 schemes: No. 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8, 11, 12, 14, 15, 18, 19, 22, and 24. The comparison results are shown in Fig. 12 .

At the end of discharge, the optimized scheme No. 30 has a maximum cell temperature of 27.29 °C, a temperature field standard deviation of 1.87 °C, and a pressure drop of 4.12 Pa. Comparing the simulation results of schemes No. 15, No. 22, and No. 30, it is found that the maximum cell temperature of No. 30 is 2.48 % and 11.26 % higher than that of schemes No. 15 and No. 22, respectively. The maximum increase in temperature field standard deviation is 13.00 %, or 0.22 °C, while the pressure drop is reduced by 53.71 % and 45.18 %, respectively. Therefore, the thermal management system of No. 30 achieves a balanced state in terms of heat dissipation and pressure drop, thus meeting the design requirements of thermal system.
Fig. 13 shows the schematic diagram and actual photo of liquid cooling test system.

The system is mainly composed of three parts: battery module (heating module), coolant circulation loop, and electronic control unit. The heating module consists of two half cells and a cooling-plate. The simulating battery is composed of quartz glass and Kapton heaters. When a voltage signal from a regulated power supply is input into the heater in real time, it can simulate the real battery's heat generation rate. After the battery units are assembled, they are symmetrically arranged on both sides of cooling-plate. The assembly is then wrapped with aerogel felt, placed in an insulated box, and kept in a high and low temperature test chamber to maintain a constant initial temperature for the module. The coolant circulation loop consists of a thermostatic circulator, a chiller, an expansion tank, and a water pump. An electronic control unit includes such components as a flowmeter, temperature-pressure transmitters, an electronic control box, and a host computer. This unit implements the functions of online adjustment of pump operation, signal conversion and output of transmitters, signal transmission of host computer software, and data acquisition. Detailed specifications of main component within the system are shown in Table 10 .

First, the heat generation rate of heating module is calibrated, and temperature rise data of the real battery is benchmarked. Fig. 14 (a) shows the comparison curve of average temperature between the heating module and the real battery. The temperature variation trends of both groups of batteries are similar, with a maximum temperature difference of 1.53 °C and a relative error within 5.33 %, indicating high accuracy in the calibration of heat generation rate of the heating module.

Subsequently, at an initial ambient temperature of 25.0 °C, when the battery discharge rates are 0.7, 1.0, and 1.2, mass flows of GS are 0.2 × 10−3 kg s−1, 0.4 × 10−3 kg s−1, and 0.5 × 10−3 kg s−1, respectively. Two liquid cooling systems, LCP-A and LCP-C, are used for cooling the heating module. Fig. 14 compares experimental and simulated temperatures of the battery module.

In Fig. 14(b)–(d), temperature rise curves obtained from the experiment closely match the simulated temperature results. The maximum deviations between experimental and simulated results occur in the mid-discharge phase of the battery. The maximum temperature deviation is 2.22 °C, with a relative deviation within 7.56 %, indicating that the simulation results are reliable. Additionally, although the heat dissipation performance of LCP-C and LCP-A liquid cooling systems is similar, LCP-C results in a more stable temperature rise during battery discharge. This significantly improves high temperature heat dissipation and low temperature thermal insulation performance of the battery module during charge and discharge processes. There are inevitable deviations during the testing process due to the following reasons: a) The constructed thermal simulation model is inconsistent with the actual structure of battery; b) The heat generation characteristics of the heating module are different from those of the actual battery; c) There is a positional deviation between the temperature measurement points of the simulating battery module during the experiment and temperature collection points in the thermal simulation.

The reliability of experimental results needs to be represented by the uncertainty of random errors (Type An uncertainty) during the experimental process and errors generated by the instruments used (Type B uncertainty). In this study, when the temperature of Kapton heaters varies between −40 °C and 70 °C, an uncertainty of 0.6 % can be introduced. The uncertainties of other devices are shown in Table 10. According to Moffat's [63] uncertainty analysis method, the Type B uncertainty of two battery heat generation rate testing experiments can be estimated by equation (8),
(8)
Δ q
q
=
(
Δ
c cell
c cell
)
2
+
(
Δ
m cell
m cell
)
2
+
(
Δ
T cell
T cell
)
2
+
(
Δ
A cell
A cell
)
2
+
(
Δ
q ‾
q ‾
)
2

The solution shows that the uncertainties of two battery heat generation rate experiments are 2.02 % and 2.33 %, respectively, which proves the high accuracy of heat generation rate test results in this paper.

This study introduces a novel cooling-plate channel construction that couple honeycomb-like fins with streamlined main channels to provide thermal management for pouch lithium-ion batteries. Firstly, such impacts as various coolant mass flows, inlet temperatures, main channel aspect ratios, and fin spacings on battery module are studied numerically. Then, an orthogonal experiment is performed to determine the optimal matching scheme for battery module's comprehensive performance. Finally, a thermal test system is built to verify the simulation results. The main conclusions are as follows:
(1)
As coolant mass flow increases, the cell's maximum temperature and temperature difference are suppressed, but the trend of temperature reduction exhibits a marginal effect, and pressure drop of the cooling-plate increases linearly (Δp = 2.57G - 0.50). Meanwhile, At the end of discharge, the battery's temperature rises as the coolant inlet temperature rises. but uniformity of the battery temperature field is improved, and pressure drop of cooling-plate decreases linearly to 4.03 Pa.
(2)
As main channel aspect ratio and fin spacing of cooling-plate increase, the trends of battery's maximum and minimum temperatures are opposite. The pressure drop and mass of the cooling-plate decrease. However, when fin spacing expands, the greatest deviation in pressure drop is 1.03 Pa, and uniformity of coolant flow distribution in the cooling-plate is weakened.
(3)
A5B2C2D3 is the optimal parameter matching scheme for comprehensive performance by orthogonal experiments. With this design, the pressure drop could be reduced by up to 53.71 %, the maximum cell temperature increased by 11.26 %, and the temperature field standard deviation increased by 13.00 %.
(4)
The LCP-C liquid cooling system demonstrates superior heat dissipation performance, with a more gradual overall temperature change in the battery. And the experiment's minimum temperature deviation is 0.49 °C, the largest relative deviation is 7.56 %.

Linxiang Fu: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Methodology. Zhendong Zhang: Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Lei Sheng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zhiwei Kuang: Validation, Software. Zehua Zhu: Validation, Software, Formal analysis. Qing Bi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52472381 and 52206267), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021M702191), and High Performance Computing Centre at the University of Shanghai for Science and Technology.
